home-intro-hook: A way of building web applications that removes abstractions instead of adding them.
home-intro-p1: Patterns you can copy, read, and own — not a black box you install and hope works.
home-intro-p2: This site is built with its own patterns. Everything you see here is a standalone module.
home-why-p1: Frameworks solved two problems at once: technical modularity and workforce standardization. The industry conflated these. Frameworks won because they made developers interchangeable — not because they were technically superior.
home-why-p2: The web platform matured while everyone was locked in. ES modules, Proxy, History API, Fetch, async/await — the browser already has what frameworks re-invented.
home-why-p3: AI dissolves the workforce problem. You no longer need 8 interchangeable devs. You need 1-2 experienced people who understand what they're building.
home-why-p4: AI itself doesn't want frameworks. Given full autonomy, AI agents choose zero-dependency solutions — every framework is a liability, a context burden, a version conflict waiting to happen. Frameworks exist to standardize humans. AI doesn't need standardizing.
home-how-li1: Factory functions, not classes. No new, no this for state, no constructor.
home-how-li2: Closures for private state. The returned object is the public API.
home-how-li3: Single-file modules. Copy one file, it works. Copy ten, they work together.
home-how-li4: Minimal dependencies. If you need a date library, use one — but understand why, and consider building just the part you need. With AI, rebuilding the 20% you actually use is faster than inheriting the 80% you don't.
home-proof-p1: This site is the proof. Router, authentication with real 2FA, data tables with inline editing, localisation — all vanilla JavaScript.
home-gain-li4: No framework churn, no migration guides
home-gain-li5: No abstraction tax — when something breaks, you read your code
home-gain-li6: Regulatory clarity — in medtech, fintech, owning your stack means owning your audit trail
home-lose-intro: Every framework and every npm dependency is a liability — a version to maintain, a breaking change to absorb, an abstraction to debug through, an SBOM entry to justify. The more you depend on, the less you own.
home-lose-conventions-p1: Frameworks prevent chaos in large teams. But AI agents don't need conventions to stay consistent — they read the codebase, not the style guide.
home-lose-ecosystem-p1: No off-the-shelf component libraries. We provide examples you can use as starting points, but we strongly encourage you to build your own. With AI, building the 20% you need takes less time than managing the 80% you don't.
home-lose-hiring-p1: Everyone knows React. But in the era of AI, framework knowledge is depreciating. What matters is understanding JavaScript and the platform. AI handles the rest.
home-closing-p1: In the AI era, move down from high-level abstractions — or you'll be locking AI to your abstraction needs.
home-closing-p2: Learn the underlying technology. Hold your ground. Own your creation.
principles-intro: These are not rules. They are preferences — shaped by experience, dyslexia, and the belief that the simplest approach is usually the most durable.
principles-clean-dom-p1: The HTML your components generate should be as pristine as possible. No inline styles. No Tailwind class pollution. No generated content injected by CSS-in-JS libraries like styled-components.
principles-clean-dom-p2: When you inspect the DOM, you should see structure — not noise. A <div class="field"> tells you what it is. A <div class="css-1a2b3c"> tells you nothing.
principles-clean-dom-p3: Styles belong in SCSS files, scoped by the component's root class. The DOM is for structure, CSS is for presentation. Keep them separate.
principles-tag-p1: Every Alkimia component stamps its root element with a data-alkimia-* attribute. This creates a direct link between what you see in DevTools and the file that generated it.
principles-tag-li2: data-alkimia-service="Router" → a service with a visual presence
principles-tag-p2: The attribute name is the category. The value is the component name. Open the inspector, find the node, know the file. No guessing, no searching.
principles-factory-p1: Every component is a function that returns an object. No class, no new, no this for state. Private state lives in closures. The returned object is the public API — explicit, intentional, readable.
principles-verbose-p1: A 20-line function that reads like prose is better than a 5-line version that requires mental unpacking. This is a dyslexia-informed design choice — but it produces better code for everyone.
principles-deps-p1: If you need a library, use one. But understand why, and consider building just the part you need. Every dependency is a version to maintain, a breaking change to absorb, an SBOM entry to justify.
router-intro: A self-contained SPA router. Builds its own nav, manages pages, optionally owns the address bar. The same component powers the top navigation of this site and the sidebar you are reading right now.
router-how-intro: A router is three things: a nav element, a page container, and a resolve function that connects them.
router-step1-p1: Each route is an entry in a plain object. The key is the route ID. The value carries a label, a path, and a page factory.
router-step1-p2: The page factory is a function that returns an object with a view property — a DOM element. The router calls it once, on first visit.
router-step2-p1: Loop over routes, create links. Each link calls navigateTo() on click.
router-step3-p1: Match the path to a route. Create the page if it doesn't exist. Hide the previous, show the current.
router-step3-p2: Pages persist in the DOM. No destroy/recreate. Scroll position survives navigation naturally.
router-step4-p1: If this router owns the URL, add two things: push state on navigate, listen to popstate for back/forward.
router-step4-p2: This is a configuration choice, not a code difference. The same router component works with or without the address bar — controlled by a single flag.
router-step5-p1: A second instance of the same component, without the address bar flag. Driven by events from the parent.
router-step5-p2: A module-level guard prevents two routers from claiming the address bar. Second attempt throws.
router-source-p1: Single file, zero dependencies. Inspect the playground for a working isolated example:
auth-intro: Try it. Register an account, scan the QR code with your authenticator app, then log in with 2FA. Everything below is live — backed by a mock server with real TOTP validation.
auth-hint: Use any email and password. The 2FA code comes from your authenticator app after scanning the QR code.
auth-how-intro: This is not a package. What follows is the mental model — enough to build your own from scratch. The full source is linked at the bottom.
auth-step1-p1: Authentication is a state machine. The component holds a state string and a view container. When state changes, the view swaps.
auth-step2-p1: Like every component: a factory function that returns an instance. Services are passed in — never imported globally.
auth-step3-p1: Each state has a build function that creates its DOM. The render function clears the container and appends the current state's view. Views are cached on first build — navigating back doesn't recreate them.
auth-step3-p2: This is the morphing pattern: same component, different visual form based on state. No conditional rendering, no virtual DOM diffing — just swap the element.
auth-step4-p1: Each form field is an Input instance with validators attached. Validators are regular functions called with the widget as this — they control the visual feedback directly.
auth-step4-p2: markInvalid() and markValid() propagate upward — a form can listen via onValidityChange() to enable or disable its submit button.
auth-step5-p1: Plain fetch. No wrapper library. The response drives the state transition.
auth-step6-p1: The server generates a TOTP secret per account and returns a QR code as SVG. The client just injects it. No client-side crypto needed.
auth-step6-p2: The user scans with any authenticator app. Verification is one call: send the 6-digit code, the server validates against the stored secret.
auth-deps-p1: Three client files, zero npm dependencies. Server-side uses two packages for TOTP — replace with your language's equivalent.
auth-source-p1: Inspect the complete implementation, including the playground and mock server:
input-intro: Text, email, and password fields with built-in validation, visual feedback, and password visibility toggle. Validators are configuration — the input does not know what "valid" means, you define it.
input-how-intro: An input component is a factory that creates a label, a field container, and an issue display. Validators are passed in as configuration — the component provides the visual API, you provide the rules.
input-step1-p1: A function that returns a view and a public API. The DOM is built once in _init().
input-step2-p1: Each validator declares which event triggers it and a function that runs. The function receives the input value and is called with the component as this.
input-step2-p2: The component does not know what "valid" means. It only knows how to show red, orange, or grey — you decide when.
input-step3-p1: Three issue types drive the styling. The field border changes on markValid/markInvalid.
input-step4-p1: A parent form listens to validity changes to enable or disable its submit button.
input-step4-p2: Each input is independent. The form composes them — no shared validation state, no form-level validator.
input-step5-p1: Pass an IconService and set type to 'password'. The eye/eyeSlash toggle is built automatically.
input-source-p1: Single file, zero dependencies (IconService optional for password toggle):
datatable-intro: Sort by clicking column headers. Filter by typing. Double-click a row to edit inline. 500 rows, 5 per page. Zero dependencies.
datatable-how-intro: A data table is a filter, a sort function, a paginator, a render loop, and an inline editing drawer. No virtual scrolling, no framework — just DOM.
datatable-step1-p1: Columns are data. Each entry declares a key, a label, whether it's sortable, and whether it's editable in the drawer.
datatable-step2-p1: One input, one loop. Check every column of every row against the filter text. Reset to page 1 on each keystroke.
datatable-step3-p1: Click a header, toggle direction. Numbers compare numerically, strings use localeCompare.
datatable-step4-p1: Two modes. Paginated slices the array and renders one page. Infinite scroll appends batches as the user scrolls down. Both use a scroll container with a sticky header.
datatable-step5-p1: Each data row is followed by a hidden drawer row. Double-click toggles it. The drawer spans all columns and contains label + input pairs for each editable field.
datatable-step5-p2: Drawer state is tracked by row ID — expanded drawers survive filter and page changes.
datatable-step6-p1: The save button collects edited values and calls an onSave callback. The callback receives the updated data and a done() function to confirm the save and update the display row.
transfer-intro: One function. ~15 lines. Our preferred way to handle parameters, defaults, and dependency injection. You don't need it — spread and destructuring work fine. But we like what it gives us.
transfer-what-p1: Deep-merges properties from a source into a target, but only for keys that already exist in the target.
transfer-why-row1-spread: Accepted silently — typos become invisible bugs
transfer-why-row1-transfer: Ignored — only declared keys pass through
transfer-why-row2-spread: Shallow only — nested objects get replaced
transfer-why-row2-transfer: Recursive — nested objects are merged key by key
transfer-why-row3-spread: Implicit — you have to read the destructuring
transfer-why-row3-transfer: Explicit — the target object is the contract
transfer-pattern-p1: Every factory function declares its parameters as a transfer() call. The target object is the contract: here is what I accept, here are the defaults.
transfer-pattern-p2: This is dependency injection without a container. The caller decides what to provide. The component declares what it accepts. transfer() is the handshake.
